# Stoicism

Stoicism is a school of Hellenistic philosophy founded in Athens by Zeno of
Citium around 300 BCE. The name derives from the Stoa Poikile, or "painted
porch," a colonnade in the Athenian agora where Zeno and his followers gathered
to teach.

The central claim of Stoic ethics is that virtue is the only true good and is
sufficient for happiness. The Stoics held that a good life is one lived in
accordance with nature and reason. Destructive emotions, they argued, result
from errors in judgment, and a person of practical wisdom would not suffer them.

A core Stoic doctrine is the dichotomy of control: some things are within our
power (our judgments, desires, and actions) and some are not (external events,
the opinions of others, our reputation). Tranquility comes from focusing only on
what is within our control and accepting what is not.

Prominent later Stoics include the Roman statesman Seneca, the former slave
Epictetus, and the emperor Marcus Aurelius, whose private notebook is known as
the Meditations. Stoic ideas strongly influenced later Western thought and the
modern practice of cognitive behavioral therapy.
